content:
{
  "title": "Self-Contradiction Prompting: Enhancing LLM Robustness to Adversarial Instructions via Consistency Checks",
  "problem": "Large language models are vulnerable to adversarial attacks and jailbreak prompts that elicit unsafe or policy-violating outputs. Existing prompt engineering and alignment strategies struggle against subtle or unseen attack variations.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are increasingly deployed in sensitive domains, robustly defending against adversarial misuse is critical. Recent jailbreaks reveal that adaptive attackers can circumvent static prompt defenses, motivating dynamic, context-aware mitigation strategies.",
  "method": "We propose Self-Contradiction Prompting (SCP), a method that augments the user’s prompt with an auto-generated counterfactual instruction (e.g., 'Describe why it would be unsafe to comply with the above request') and requires the model to answer both the original and counterfactual in parallel. By evaluating the consistency between these responses, the model can be steered to detect and resist adversarial intent. The counterfactual is generated by a secondary LLM or a prompt template optimized to surface policy conflicts.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate SCP on the AdvBench and JailbreakEval datasets, measuring attack success rate and harmlessness agreement. Baselines include vanilla prompting and state-of-the-art adversarial defense prompts (e.g., constitutional AI, refuse/clarify chains). The key metric is reduction in attack success rates without degrading helpfulness on benign prompts."
}
